feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The European Public Policy Partnership (EPPP) is a non-governmental think tank established in 1998 with offices in Bratislava and Brussels. For over 25 years, we have represented the interests of Central and Eastern European (CEE) businesses, amplifying their perspectives on how to improve the business environment so they can focus on what they do best: creating value for customers and contributing to prosperous, innovative economies that help bridge the gap between newer and older EU member states.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Our engagement has extended to digital policy, where we have contributed to the development of landmark initiatives such as the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), and now the Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Building on this, our new quantitative study of small and medium-sized enterprise (SME) owners and senior managers from across the CEE region provides a clear, region-specific insight into how online advertising works in practice (EPPP, 2025).

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We welcome the Commission's call for evidence and see the upcoming DFA as an important opportunity to fine-tune the EU's digital regulatory framework. The EU already has strong rules in place through instruments like the GDPR, the DSA, the DMA, the ePrivacy Directive or the AI Act. Together, these form one of the most comprehensive systems of regulation in the world. For the DFA to succeed, we propose that it should focus on addressing specific gaps that remain, rather than repeating what is already covered. Adding new layers of rules could create confusion, make enforcement harder, and increase compliance costs. This would be felt most by SMEs, which already depend heavily on digital tools to compete and grow.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act is intended to address several practices that undermine consumer protection, including dark patterns, misleading marketing, and addictive design in digital products. We wish to draw particular attention to the issue of unfair personalisation practices. In this context, we outline our perspective and provide targeted recommendations on how the new legislation can address this area effectively.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: According to our latest survey, personalized online advertising is a routine and essential tool for SMEs across Central and Eastern Europe. Across six markets – Slovakia, Czechia, Poland, Hungary, Lithuania, and Croatia – SMEs consistently report tangible benefits from online advertising. In every market, at least three-quarters of respondents can link – at least to some extent – online advertising with measurable business outcomes such as revenue growth or new customer acquisition. The most frequently cited gains include new customer acquisition (around $66\%$ ), better local reach ( $\approx 43\%$ ), and higher sales ( $\approx 34\%$ ) (EPPP, 2025).

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In practice, most SMEs make active use of targeted advertising, with usage averaging at 67% across the region. About half of respondents report that at least a quarter of their revenue is driven by targeted online advertising. Strikingly, over 80% are also inclined to say that online ads help them compete with larger or more established firms. Therefore, it does not come as a surprise that nearly half of SMEs believe that allowing legislative changes that would make targeting illegal or overly difficult would negatively affect both customer acquisition and overall business performance (EPPP, 2025).

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The Digital Services Act already regulates malign advertising by limiting the targeting of sensitive data and advertising to minors. We fully recognise and support that protecting young people and sensitive data must remain a top priority. At the same time, the findings in our report indicate the importance of proportionate, consent-based targeting. Enforcement should therefore continue to focus on high-risk areas while maintaining lawful targeting options for SMEs. This approach will allow small businesses to rely on responsible, consent-driven personalization to compete effectively and grow.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The theoretical section of our report also highlighted the issue of the regulatory environment being both crowded and constantly evolving. It evidenced how stakeholders must navigate overlapping rules, ranging from privacy consents to platform accountability to consumer rights, which can sometimes conflict or create uncertainty. For instance, a small business running personalized ads must meet GDPR consent and cookie requirements, ensure compliance with DSA, and prepare for upcoming changes under new regulations (EPPP, 2025). This complexity has led to calls for simplification. Similarly, an IAB Europe survey (Kantar Media, 2025) found that consumers are asking for clearer rules and consistent enforcement of existing laws, rather than a growing number of new ones. This complexity highlights why the DFA must be carefully drafted, particularly in defining key concepts.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In the context of ensuring that the DFA effectively addresses unfair personalization, there is clear room for improvement to avoid adding complexity to the digital landscape. A central challenge is preventing definitions from being too expansive, as this can create legal uncertainty and risk excessive enforcement. The DFA aims to prevent practices that exploit consumer vulnerabilities. Therefore, it is essential that fundamental concepts, such as the definition of vulnerability, must be carefully crafted. If vulnerability is defined too widely, for example including "advertising causing emotional distress," restrictions could become unworkable. This is especially true for automated advertising systems that process thousands of ads continuously. Such uncertainty may result in undue enforcement and directly harm businesses that rely on advertising. SMEs are particularly at risk, as online advertising is often their only financially viable option. Hence, we urge legislators to ensure that the DFA uses clear and objective categories of data to support a competitive digital economy.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: To conclude, the Digital Fairness Act offers a clear opportunity to strengthen Europe's digital regulatory framework. To succeed, however, it must focus on addressing genuine gaps, guided by real-world evidence and avoid duplicating rules. In the Act's agenda to address unfair personalisation practices, protection of minors and sensitive data should remain paramount. At the same time, it is essential that SMEs retain access to proportionate and consent-based tools that enable them to compete and grow. Our report, which surveyed SMEs across Central and Eastern Europe, demonstrates the real-life experience of business owners who confirm that personalised advertising is an essential tool for their success. We therefore urge legislators to approach the regulation of personalisation practices with caution and to focus specifically on addressing unfair uses rather than limiting tools that support SME competitiveness. To achieve this balance, the Act must rely on precise and objective definitions that minimise legal uncertainty, reduce compliance burdens, and avoid disproportionate enforcement. By doing so, the DFA can enhance consumer protection while safeguarding innovation and ensuring a fair digital landscape.

section_path: European Public Policy Partnership's Feedback on the Digital Fairness Act
